overview.title: The aix language
overview.lead: aix is a tiny declarative language for backends. You declare entities (data shapes) and routes (exposed actions); a fixed runtime interprets the spec as a live CRUD API. No code is generated — and a deterministic verifier proves a spec correct before it ever runs.
overview.twoKinds: Every .aix file has one declaration per line, and exactly two kinds. Braces are optional; # starts a comment.
overview.next: Start with Entities & fields, or jump to how aix compares to other tools.

entities.title: Entities & fields
entities.lead: Fields are comma-separated and follow a fixed left-to-right order, so a machine parses them 100% deterministically.

parts.:ref: for a ref, the entity it points to

types.ts: timestamp (=now, or the shorthand name@, sets creation time)
types.enum[a|b|c]: a closed set of allowed values

routes.title: Routes
routes.lead: The entity must already be defined. A route lists the actions to expose over HTTP.
routes.infer: list:mine implies both auth and private — you don't write them. A standalone private implies auth too. Less to type, and the coupled tokens can't be forgotten.

runtime.title: Runtime rules
runtime.lead: How the fixed runtime interprets a verified spec, per request:
rules: Auth — with auth, a request missing the x-user-id header gets 401.
Owner injection — create always sets the owner ref to the logged-in user; a client cannot spoof it.
Owner scope (private) — get/update/delete on another user's row returns 404 (no existence leak).
Referential integrity — a non-owner ref must point at an existing row, else 400.
Validation — required, type, max length/value, and enum membership are enforced (400).
Unique — a ~ field rejects a duplicate value with 409.
Field lock — changing a field not in update:[...] returns 403.
Defaults — applied when a field is absent; now / @ become the current ISO timestamp.
Queries — filter (equality, declared fields only) then sort then page.

errors.title: Verifier error codes
errors.lead: aix check judges a spec 100% by machine. If it returns no errors, the runtime is guaranteed to run it. Typos get a deterministic "did you mean …?" hint.

codes.DUP_FIELD: duplicate field name in one entity
codes.BAD_REF: ref target is not a defined entity
codes.BAD_DEFAULT: default doesn't match the type (=now is ts-only; an enum default must be a member)
codes.BAD_MAX: <= used on a type other than str/int
codes.BAD_UNIQUE: ~ used on a type other than str/int
codes.MULTI_OWNER: more than one * owner in an entity
codes.NO_ENTITY: route points at an undefined entity
codes.BAD_UPDATE: update:[X] where X is not a real field
codes.OWNER_LOCKED: the owner ref cannot be in update:[...] (ownership is immutable)
codes.PRIVATE_LIST: private together with a public list — use list:mine
codes.OWNER_CREATE_NO_AUTH: create with an owner ref needs auth so the owner can be injected
codes.FILTER_FIELD: filter:[X] where X is not a real field
codes.SORT_FIELD: sort field is missing or not str/int/ts
codes.NO_OWNER: list:mine/private but the entity has no ref
codes.AMBIGUOUS_OWNER: list:mine/private with multiple refs and no * marker

compare.title: aix vs other tools
compare.lead: aix is a small language, not a library. The same owner-scoped blog backend — login, ownership, validation, field-locked updates — is three lines of aix. The conventional stacks below are real and kept tight; the bulk is imperative glue a machine cannot prove correct.
compare.colSame: the same backend, written the usual way

items.name: Prisma + Next.js
items.note: A Prisma schema, plus one App-Router route handler per operation with zod validation, auth checks, and ownership scoping all written by hand.
items.takeaway: ~300 tokens across 5 files of glue — every line a place to get auth or ownership wrong, with no static check that you didn't.

items.name: Ruby on Rails
items.note: A migration, an ActiveRecord model, a controller with strong params, a before_action for auth, and manual scoping to current_user.
items.takeaway: Convention removes some boilerplate, but ownership and field-locking are still hand-written policy.

items.name: Django + DRF
items.note: A model, a serializer, a ViewSet, a permission class, and a get_queryset scoped to the request user.
items.takeaway: Five moving parts to wire correctly for what aix states in a single line.

items.name: Raw SQL + handlers
items.note: DDL, plus a hand-written endpoint per operation doing its own parsing, validation, and WHERE owner = ? scoping.
items.takeaway: Maximum control — and maximum surface for a silent authorization bug.

compare.closing: In every stack, the validation and authorization are imperative code that no tool checks for correctness. In aix they are declarations a deterministic verifier proves before the API ever serves a request — which is the whole point of making it a language.
